wizards.name: Prompt Engineering
wizards.desc: Practical techniques for writing prompts that produce reliable, high-quality LLM output.
wizards.author: Lore Library

notes.title: Clear, Specific Instructions
notes.body:
# Clear, Specific Instructions

The single highest-leverage habit in prompting is removing ambiguity. Models do not infer unstated intent reliably, so state exactly what you want, in what format, and for whom.

**Practical rules:**

- Lead with the task, then constraints, then context. Put the most important instruction first.
- Replace vague verbs ("analyze", "improve") with concrete asks ("list three risks", "rewrite in under 50 words").
- Specify the output shape explicitly: a bulleted list, a table, a JSON object. See [[Structured Output]].
- Give the model a persona or audience when tone matters ("explain to a non-technical manager").
- Prefer positive instructions ("respond only with the SQL query") over negatives, which models follow less reliably.

**Anti-patterns:** stacking many loosely related requests in one paragraph, relying on the model to guess length or style, and burying the actual question under context. When a prompt underperforms, the fix is usually more precision, not more words.

Clarity also compounds with other techniques: a well-scoped instruction makes [[Few-Shot Prompting]] examples easier to write and [[Iteration & Evaluation]] far easier to measure.

_Source: https://www.promptingguide.ai/introduction/tips_

notes.title: Few-Shot Prompting
notes.body:
# Few-Shot Prompting

Few-shot prompting steers a model by showing it a handful of input/output examples before the real query. The model infers the pattern and continues it, which is far more reliable than describing the pattern in prose alone.

**When it helps:**

- Enforcing a specific output format or labeling scheme.
- Tasks where the rule is easier to demonstrate than to explain.
- Domain-specific tone, classification, or extraction.

**How to do it well:**

- Use 2-5 diverse examples that cover edge cases, not five near-duplicates. Diversity prepares the model for variety in real inputs.
- Keep the example format identical to what you want back, character for character.
- Order matters less than coverage, but put a clean, canonical example first.
- For reasoning-heavy tasks, combine examples with worked reasoning steps; this is the bridge to [[Chain-of-Thought Reasoning]].

**Limits:** few-shot consumes context budget and can overfit to the exact examples shown. If formatting alone is the goal, a [[System Prompts & Role Setting]] directive may be cheaper. If accuracy on multi-step problems is the goal, add explicit reasoning.

_Source: https://www.promptingguide.ai/techniques/fewshot_

notes.title: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
notes.body:
# Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, introduced by Wei et al. (2022), unlocks complex reasoning by having the model produce intermediate steps before its final answer. Instead of jumping to a conclusion, the model "shows its work," which sharply improves accuracy on arithmetic, logic, and multi-hop questions.

**Two main flavors:**

- **Few-shot CoT:** provide examples where each answer includes the reasoning chain, then ask the new question. Pairs naturally with [[Few-Shot Prompting]].
- **Zero-shot CoT:** simply append "Let's think step by step." Kojima et al. (2022) showed this alone fixes many problems the model otherwise gets wrong, with no examples needed.

**Why it works:** decomposing a problem gives the model intermediate anchors, reducing the chance of a single leap-of-logic error. The benefit is strongest in larger models, where the authors describe reasoning as an emergent ability.

**Cautions:** the visible reasoning can itself contain mistakes, so for high-stakes use, verify the final answer rather than trusting the chain. CoT also adds tokens and latency, so reserve it for genuinely multi-step tasks. For production, you can ask for hidden reasoning and a clean final answer in [[Structured Output]].

_Source: https://www.promptingguide.ai/techniques/cot_

notes.title: System Prompts & Role Setting
notes.body:
# System Prompts & Role Setting

The system prompt sets durable, conversation-wide behavior: identity, scope, tone, and hard rules. Unlike a single user turn, it persists across the exchange and frames every response, so it is the right place for constraints you never want violated.

**What belongs in a system prompt:**

- Role and expertise ("You are a precise enterprise support assistant").
- Boundaries and refusals (what the assistant must not do).
- Default format and tone, so individual prompts stay short. Reinforce with [[Clear, Specific Instructions]].
- Tool and policy rules for agentic setups.

**Best practices:**

- Keep it stable; treat it as configuration, not a scratchpad.
- State priorities explicitly so the model knows what wins when instructions conflict (e.g., safety over helpfulness).
- Avoid contradictions between the system prompt and user instructions; the model may resolve them unpredictably.
- Test the system prompt against adversarial inputs that try to override it.

A strong system prompt reduces repetition in every downstream turn and makes outputs more consistent, which in turn makes [[Iteration & Evaluation]] meaningful because behavior is no longer drifting turn to turn.

_Source: https://www.promptingguide.ai/introduction/tips_

notes.title: Structured Output
notes.body:
# Structured Output

When another program consumes the model's response, free-form prose is a liability. Ask for structured output, typically JSON, so results are parseable and verifiable.

**Techniques:**

- Provide an explicit schema or a filled example of the exact shape you want, then say "respond only with valid JSON matching this structure."
- Name every field and its type; ambiguity in the schema becomes ambiguity in the output.
- For reasoning tasks, separate a `reasoning` field from a `answer` field so you keep the benefits of [[Chain-of-Thought Reasoning]] without polluting the machine-readable result.
- Use a single canonical example as a one-shot anchor, a light form of [[Few-Shot Prompting]].

**Guardrails:**

- Always validate the returned JSON; never assume it parses. Reject and retry on failure.
- Constrain enums explicitly ("status must be one of: open, closed, pending").
- Keep schemas flat where possible; deeply nested structures raise error rates.

Structured output is what turns a chat model into a dependable component in a pipeline, and it is a precondition for automated [[Iteration & Evaluation]] because you can score fields directly.

_Source: https://www.promptingguide.ai/introduction/tips_

notes.title: Iteration & Evaluation
notes.body:
# Iteration & Evaluation

Prompting is empirical. The first prompt is rarely the best one, so treat prompt development like engineering: change one variable, measure, and keep what wins.

**A practical loop:**

1. Assemble a small but representative test set of real inputs with known good outputs.
2. Write a baseline prompt and run it across the set.
3. Inspect failures and categorize them: wrong format, missing reasoning, hallucination, tone.
4. Make one targeted change, often adding [[Clear, Specific Instructions]], an example via [[Few-Shot Prompting]], or [[Chain-of-Thought Reasoning]].
5. Re-run and compare. Avoid changing several things at once, which hides what helped.

**Measurement tips:**

- Prefer objective checks (does the JSON parse, is the label correct) over vibes. [[Structured Output]] makes this possible.
- Track regressions; a change that fixes one case can break three others.
- Log prompts and outputs so improvements are reproducible.

The goal is not a clever prompt but a reliable one. Disciplined evaluation is what separates a demo that works once from a prompt that holds up across thousands of varied inputs.

_Source: https://www.promptingguide.ai/introduction/tips_

wizards.name: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
wizards.desc: Production retrieval engineering: chunking, embeddings, hybrid search, reranking, and evaluation.
wizards.author: Lore Library

notes.title: Chunking Strategy
notes.body:
# Chunking Strategy

If you fix only one thing in a RAG system, fix chunking. Chunks are the units of knowledge your system can retrieve, so bad chunking caps quality no matter how good your embeddings or reranker are.

Chunking controls two things at once: **recall** (does the answer-bearing text live inside a single retrievable chunk) and **precision** (is that chunk tight enough to be useful without dragging in unrelated content). Too large and chunks become "topic soup"; too small and you lose the definitions and context that make a passage interpretable.

**Sensible defaults to test:**

- Chunk sizes of 256 / 512 / 1024 tokens, measured against Recall@K and MRR.
- Overlap of 10-20%; increase it when documents have many cross-references or definitions near section boundaries.
- Prefix each chunk with its document title and H1/H2/H3 path, then the body. Queries often match the heading frame better than body text alone.

**Strategies:** fixed-size with overlap (baseline), structure-aware splitting for Markdown/HTML/sectioned PDFs, semantic chunking on topic boundaries, and small-to-big (parent-child) retrieval. Always store stable chunk IDs plus metadata so you can filter, cite, and audit.

Good chunks feed everything downstream, including [[Embeddings & Index Design]] and [[Hybrid Search]].

_Source: https://www.stackai.com/insights/retrieval-augmented-generation-(rag)-best-practices-for-enterprise-ai-chunking-embeddings-reranking-and-hybrid-search-optimization_

notes.title: Embeddings & Index Design
notes.body:
# Embeddings & Index Design

Once chunking is sane, embeddings determine how well you generate semantic candidates. But swapping embedding models is rarely the first fix; most retrieval failures trace back to chunking, missing metadata filters, or mixed corpora in one index.

**What embeddings are good and bad at:** they excel at semantic similarity but struggle with exact matches (IDs, SKUs, error codes), rare jargon, and negation ("not supported", "except"). That weakness is exactly why pure vector search misses "obvious" answers, and why you pair it with [[Hybrid Search]].

**Selection criteria (enterprise lens):** domain fit, context length and long-input behavior, multilingual needs, cost and ingestion throughput, and security/data-residency posture, not leaderboard vibes.

**What to embed:** a common mistake is embedding only body text. Better is title + headings + body, which aligns chunk vectors with how people actually phrase questions. Preserve exact tokens (versions, region codes, SKUs) as metadata and lexical fields rather than hoping vectors capture them.

**Index tuning:** pick the right similarity metric, tune ANN parameters (HNSW/IVF), and size your candidate set. A practical pattern is to retrieve 50-200 candidates cheaply, then narrow to 5-12 with [[Reranking]]. Quality chunks from your [[Chunking Strategy]] remain the precondition for all of this.

_Source: https://www.stackai.com/insights/retrieval-augmented-generation-(rag)-best-practices-for-enterprise-ai-chunking-embeddings-reranking-and-hybrid-search-optimization_

notes.title: Hybrid Search
notes.body:
# Hybrid Search

Hybrid search, combining lexical BM25 with vector retrieval, is often the single biggest step-change for enterprise RAG. It pairs the semantic coverage of vectors with the exact-match reliability of keyword search.

**Why you need both:** vector retrieval struggles with rare tokens (SKUs, invoice IDs, error codes), exact phrasing requirements, and queries with multiple entities and constraints. BM25 handles those well but fails when the query is high-level or phrased differently from the source. Running both reduces both failure modes.

**Recommended pipeline:**

1. Run BM25 and vector retrieval in parallel.
2. Fuse the two ranked lists with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), a strong default because it avoids fragile score calibration between systems (k=60 is a common starting point).
3. Send the fused candidates to [[Reranking]].
4. Select the final top-K for context construction.

**Boosts that matter:** field boosts, phrase boosts for quoted strings and detected IDs, and metadata filtering (product, region, version, ACL labels) applied before reranking. Alternatives to RRF, such as weighted blending, require tuning and are more brittle.

Hybrid search depends on a solid [[Chunking Strategy]] and complementary [[Embeddings & Index Design]]; it closes the gap between "topically similar" and "actually contains the answer."

_Source: https://www.stackai.com/insights/retrieval-augmented-generation-(rag)-best-practices-for-enterprise-ai-chunking-embeddings-reranking-and-hybrid-search-optimization_

notes.title: Reranking
notes.body:
# Reranking

Reranking is where many enterprise RAG systems become production-grade. Retrieval gives you candidates; reranking decides which ones actually deserve a place in the model's limited context window.

**Why a second stage:** embedding retrieval is typically a bi-encoder, encoding query and passage separately and comparing vectors. It is fast but coarse. A **cross-encoder reranker** evaluates the query and passage jointly, which yields far better relevance for queries with constraints (region, plan, effective date), nuanced policy language, multi-part questions, or "what's the exception to..." phrasing. It is often the difference between topically similar and answer-bearing.

**Operational defaults:**

- Retrieve 50-200 candidates (ideally fused via [[Hybrid Search]]).
- Rerank the candidate set with a cross-encoder.
- Keep the top 5-12 for context, preferring shorter, coherent passages.

**Reranker options:** cross-encoder (quality default), late-interaction/ColBERT-style rerankers, and LLM-as-reranker (powerful but slow and costly, use sparingly).

**Failure modes:** overweighting topical similarity over answer relevance, latency blow-ups, and duplicate chunks crowding the final context. Apply metadata filters and dedup before reranking. Reranking is what turns the recall produced by [[Chunking Strategy]] and [[Embeddings & Index Design]] into precision, and its lift should be measured directly in [[Evaluation & Golden Sets]].

_Source: https://www.stackai.com/insights/retrieval-augmented-generation-(rag)-best-practices-for-enterprise-ai-chunking-embeddings-reranking-and-hybrid-search-optimization_

notes.title: Evaluation & Golden Sets
notes.body:
# Evaluation & Golden Sets

Without evaluation, every RAG "improvement" becomes a debate and regressions sneak in during model swaps, ingestion changes, or index migrations. Mature systems treat evaluation as a continuous measurement layer, not a one-off test.

**Build a golden set:** start with 50-200 real user queries, ideally high-volume ones from support, enablement, or policy lookups where the "right" answer is clear and the business impact is highest. Label the answer-bearing passages for each.

**Retrieval metrics that map to user outcomes:** Recall@K, MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank), and nDCG@K. These let you attribute gains to [[Chunking Strategy]], [[Embeddings & Index Design]], [[Hybrid Search]], or [[Reranking]] without conflating retrieval with generation.

**Grounding checks:** retrieval is necessary but not sufficient. Add citation coverage and a faithfulness/hallucination rate, or the model will compensate for thin evidence with fluent guessing.

**Stage-level measurement:** track BM25 recall, vector recall, fused recall after RRF, rerank lift, and latency p50/p95/p99. This is how you avoid vague conclusions like "the new embedding model seems worse" and pinpoint where a regression actually came from.

**Release discipline:** gate changes with offline eval, then canary, then A/B, then full rollout with monitoring.

_Source: https://www.stackai.com/insights/retrieval-augmented-generation-(rag)-best-practices-for-enterprise-ai-chunking-embeddings-reranking-and-hybrid-search-optimization_

notes.title: Production Operations
notes.body:
# Production Operations

A RAG system is an operational system. The moment it touches internal knowledge, customer data, or regulated content, it needs controls that scale beyond a demo.

**Access control at retrieval time:** enforce permissions during retrieval, not after generation. Use per-user or per-group metadata filtering, tenant isolation and index partitioning, and explicit data boundaries so a chunk is never surfaced to someone who shouldn't see it.

**Freshness and ingestion SLAs:** stale context is a silent failure. Teams notice hallucinations but miss "accurate answer from last quarter." Use incremental indexing and scheduled syncs, document versioning with effective dates, a rollback plan for ingestion bugs, and tight SLAs for high-impact sources like policies and incident runbooks.

**Observability:** if you can't see what retrieval did, you can't fix it. Log query text (redacted as needed), BM25 and vector candidates, fusion overlap, rerank scores, the final top-K, and the citations used. Dashboard the lexical/vector overlap, rerank gain, and "no evidence found" rate. This observability is what makes [[Evaluation & Golden Sets]] actionable.

**Cost controls:** cache retrieval results for repeated queries, rerank only when needed, and right-size candidate sets so the [[Reranking]] stage stays affordable under peak load.

_Source: https://www.stackai.com/insights/retrieval-augmented-generation-(rag)-best-practices-for-enterprise-ai-chunking-embeddings-reranking-and-hybrid-search-optimization_

wizards.name: AI Agents
wizards.desc: How LLM agents reason, use tools, plan, remember, coordinate, and stay safe in production.
wizards.author: Lore Library

notes.title: The Agent Loop
notes.body:
# The Agent Loop

An LLM agent turns a language model from a text generator into a decision-making engine. Instead of running a fixed script, the agent interprets intent, reasons about what to do, takes an action, observes the result, and repeats until the goal is met.

Concretely, given a request like "tell me why my invoice was late and credit me if it was your fault," an agent decomposes the goal into sub-tasks (look up the invoice, analyze the delay, determine responsibility, issue a credit) and decides each step dynamically, with no hard-coded branch for that exact request. This flexibility is the whole point, and also the risk: the same loop can hallucinate data, pick the wrong tool from a subtle prompt mismatch, or take an action policy forbids.

**The core cycle:**

1. Interpret the user's intent.
2. Plan or pick the next step (see [[Planning & Task Decomposition]]).
3. Call a tool or produce output (see [[Tool Use & Function Calling]]).
4. Observe the result and update state in [[Memory]].
5. Decide whether to continue or finish.

Because each step is non-deterministic, agents need oversight woven in, not bolted on. [[Guardrails & Evaluation]] validate actions in real time and analyze traces after the fact, so the autonomy that makes the loop powerful does not make it unaccountable.

_Source: https://www.patronus.ai/ai-agent-development/ai-agent-architecture_

notes.title: Tool Use & Function Calling
notes.body:
# Tool Use & Function Calling

Tools are how an agent acts on the world, querying databases, calling APIs, sending email, running code. The model selects a tool and arguments; your runtime executes it and returns the result into the loop.

**Design for reliability:**

- Define strict function-call schemas so the model must choose from a set of verifiable actions with typed parameters, rather than emitting free-form commands.
- Make tool names and descriptions unambiguous. Agents frequently invoke the wrong API simply because two endpoints have similar names.
- Apply role-aware access: an agent should only reach the tools its task legitimately needs, limiting blast radius.
- Log every tool call with inputs and outputs for observability and debugging.
- Define fallback behavior for failures, timeouts, and empty results so a single bad call doesn't derail the run.

**Failure modes to expect:** the right database is queried but the model passes an incomplete set of fields; a tool returns an error and the model hallucinates a plausible value instead; or it believes a step succeeded when it didn't. These are semantic failures, not crashes, so they need [[Guardrails & Evaluation]] to catch.

Grounding tool results back into the prompt, often via retrieval, keeps the agent's decisions tied to real data and supports the broader [[The Agent Loop]].

_Source: https://www.patronus.ai/ai-agent-development/ai-agent-architecture_

notes.title: Planning & Task Decomposition
notes.body:
# Planning & Task Decomposition

Planning is the agent's ability to break a high-level goal into an ordered set of executable steps. A planner agent might turn "generate a market report on wearables" into: search recent trends, analyze pricing, generate visualizations, write the summary.

That sounds clean, but planning is where subtle, distinctly agentic failures appear:

- The summarizer runs before analysis finishes, producing generic output.
- A downstream step receives invalid input and crashes or misleads.
- The planner assigns work to an executor that isn't initialized yet, causing a silent failure.
- A hallucinated intermediate step looks plausible but breaks the logic that follows.

**Practices that help:**

- Make dependencies explicit so a step never runs before its inputs exist.
- Keep plans inspectable; a visible plan can be validated before execution.
- Re-plan when observations contradict the current plan, rather than barreling ahead.
- Bound the number of steps to avoid runaway loops.

Planning leans heavily on [[Memory]] to carry intermediate results between steps and on [[Tool Use & Function Calling]] to execute each one. Crucially, evaluating a plan means asking not just whether steps ran, but whether they made sense, were grounded, and respected dependencies, which is the job of [[Guardrails & Evaluation]].

_Source: https://www.patronus.ai/ai-agent-development/ai-agent-architecture_

notes.title: Memory
notes.body:
# Memory

Agents operate across many loosely coupled components, and memory is what lets them carry state instead of starting from scratch each turn. Inconsistent or missing memory is a recognized failure mode in its own right.

**Common types:**

- **Working/short-term memory:** the current task context, intermediate tool results, and the running plan. This is what [[Planning & Task Decomposition]] reads and writes between steps.
- **Episodic memory:** records of past runs and tool-usage patterns, useful for adapting behavior over time.
- **Semantic memory:** durable facts and human feedback about how the agent should behave.
- **Long-term retrieval memory:** an external store (often a vector database) the agent queries to ground decisions in trusted data.

**Design considerations:**

- Decide explicitly what gets remembered, for how long, and what gets summarized or dropped, since context windows are finite.
- Track which memory informed which action so failures are traceable.
- Beware forgotten instructions: a hallucination late in a run often traces back to a misremembered earlier tool response or a dropped constraint.

Reliable memory is what makes a multi-step agent coherent, and it underpins both [[The Agent Loop]] and post-hoc [[Guardrails & Evaluation]], which often inspect the memory trail to diagnose why a run went wrong.

_Source: https://www.patronus.ai/ai-agent-development/ai-agent-architecture_

notes.title: Multi-Agent Systems
notes.body:
# Multi-Agent Systems

If a single LLM agent is an intelligent individual, a multi-agent system is a distributed organization: specialized agents that collaborate, delegate, and review each other toward a shared goal. A product launch might use a research agent, a planning agent, a content agent, and a QA agent, each prompt-engineered for its role.

**Orchestration is the hard part.** An orchestrator governs which agent acts, in what order, with which tools, and how outputs feed downstream. Beyond ordinary distributed-systems issues (two agents hitting the same database and tripping a rate limit), multi-agent setups introduce semantic failures unique to agents:

- The correct API is queried, but an agent passes an incomplete subset of fields.
- The wrong agent runs first, so a summary is generated before its inputs exist.
- A reasoning gap leads an agent to believe an upstream step was completed when it wasn't.

The final deliverable can look polished while critical insights are missing or wrong, and without introspection you'd never know.

**Make it observable.** Structured trace analysis lets you ask not just whether the pipeline ran, but whether each step was grounded and respected dependencies. Patterns like explicit message passing, dependency graphs, and a reviewing QA agent help, but they don't remove the need for [[Guardrails & Evaluation]]. Each agent still runs its own [[The Agent Loop]] with its own [[Memory]].

_Source: https://www.patronus.ai/ai-agent-development/ai-agent-architecture_

notes.title: Guardrails & Evaluation
notes.body:
# Guardrails & Evaluation

Autonomy without oversight is how agents drift into expensive mistakes. Reliable agentic systems pair two complementary strategies: real-time guardrails on each action, and structured trace analysis across the whole run.

**Guardrails (runtime checks):**

- Prompt-injection detection to catch embedded "ignore previous instructions" attacks.
- Dangerous-action prevention, e.g. blocking destructive commands before execution.
- Privacy and compliance enforcement so agents don't leak sensitive data (GDPR and similar).
- Hallucination and relevance checks comparing outputs against retrieved context.

**Why evaluation is harder for agents:** decisions are non-deterministic and span many components, memory, planning, tool selection, output, each with its own failure modes. A complete evaluation needs a broad error taxonomy covering observation, decision, and execution layers, not just "was the final answer correct."

**Trace analysis (post-hoc):** inspect the full execution to find the exact span where things went wrong, the misread tool response or forgotten instruction that caused a later error. Span-level diagnostics with root-cause insights and concrete fixes (prompt rewrites, tool reassignment) scale far better than manually reviewing thousands of traces.

Start simple with three indicators, hallucination frequency, context relevance, and output correctness, then expand. Guardrails protect [[Tool Use & Function Calling]] in the moment; trace analysis makes [[The Agent Loop]] and [[Multi-Agent Systems]] debuggable over time.

_Source: https://makersden.io/blog/ai-agent-architecture-tools-memorhy-permissions-guardrails_

wizards.name: Prediction Markets & Kalshi
wizards.desc: How prediction markets price real-world events, Kalshi mechanics, and disciplined risk concepts (educational).
wizards.author: Lore Library

notes.title: What Is a Prediction Market
notes.body:
# What Is a Prediction Market

A prediction market lets people buy and sell contracts tied to the outcome of a real-world event, from elections and economic data to weather, sports, and culture. They are also called event contracts, information markets, or idea futures, and versions have existed for centuries.

The key distinction from a traditional sportsbook: a prediction market has no stake in the outcome. It acts as a neutral middleman that matches buyers and sellers and earns money on transaction fees, much closer to a futures or stock exchange than to betting against "the house." Because the platform isn't your counterparty, a profitable trader isn't throttled or banned for winning.

Kalshi is a CFTC-regulated US exchange whose October 2024 court win against the CFTC legalized election trading in the US, helping move the whole category from the financial fringe toward the mainstream. Other venues include Polymarket (crypto-based, USDC) and PredictIt (research-oriented, with strict per-contract limits).

Contracts typically settle at $1 if the event happens and $0 if it doesn't, so the price you pay reflects an implied probability. That pricing mechanism is the heart of the system, covered in [[Reading Prices as Probabilities]], and the structures these contracts take are covered in [[Contract Types]].

_This note is educational and is not financial advice._

_Source: https://news.kalshi.com/p/how-prediction-markets-work_

notes.title: Reading Prices as Probabilities
notes.body:
# Reading Prices as Probabilities

In a binary market, a contract pays $1 if the event occurs and nothing if it doesn't. So the price, quoted between roughly 1 and 99 cents, is the market's implied probability of "Yes."

**A worked example.** "Will the US experience a government shutdown on January 31, 2026?" If "Yes" trades at 30 cents, the market implies a 30% chance: pay 30 cents to win $1 if it happens. The mirror "No" side trades at 70 cents for the same $1 payout, implying 70%. The two sides sum to about $1.

**Risk and reward are linked.** The more probable an outcome, the higher its price and the smaller the payout ratio. Unlikely outcomes are cheap but pay out large if they hit. Buying "Yes" at 30 cents risks 30 to make 70; buying it at 90 cents risks 90 to make 10.

**Prices move with information.** As traders react to news, prices, and demand shift, and the implied probability rises or falls, functioning like a continuously updated public poll. In one cited case, a Kalshi market on US measles cases surged 132% in four days as new cases were reported.

This price-as-probability framing is the foundation for evaluating value and for [[Strategy & Discipline]] and [[Risk Management]]. It applies across the structures in [[Contract Types]].

_This note is educational and is not financial advice._

_Source: https://news.kalshi.com/p/how-prediction-markets-work_

notes.title: Contract Types
notes.body:
# Contract Types

Prediction markets come in a few standard structures, each suited to a different kind of question.

- **Binary outcome markets:** two possible results, Yes or No. Contract prices reflect the probability of each, as covered in [[Reading Prices as Probabilities]]. Example: will combined points in a game exceed 48.5?
- **Multiple-selection markets:** more than two possible results, such as which candidate wins an election, which team takes a championship, or which show tops Netflix this week. Each option carries its own price.
- **Continuous forecasting markets:** instead of a winner, these price a numerical range, how high or low a quantity will go. Used heavily in finance and crypto (e.g., how high Bitcoin gets this year) and for things like total measles cases in a year.
- **Conditional markets:** forecast an outcome under a specific scenario, linking two events, "if X happens, will Y also happen?" They give direct insight into cause-and-effect relationships.

Kalshi offers a large catalog spanning politics, sports, climate, and finance, plus combo markets and a leaderboard. Polymarket and PredictIt cover overlapping topics with different rules; PredictIt notably caps individual contract volume.

Understanding which structure you're trading shapes how you read odds and size positions, the focus of [[Strategy & Discipline]] and [[Risk Management]].

_This note is educational and is not financial advice._

_Source: https://news.kalshi.com/p/how-prediction-markets-work_

notes.title: Strategy & Discipline
notes.body:
# Strategy & Discipline

The strongest prediction-market traders behave like analysts, not gamblers. A few principles recur across the educational guidance.

**Start with a plan.** Define your thesis, entry, and exit before committing capital, and track performance diligently across markets so you learn what actually works rather than relying on memory.

**Defer to data over feelings.** Charged topics, politics, elections, sports, invite emotional and motivated reasoning (trading on what you want to happen rather than what's likely). When your gut conflicts with the evidence, lean on reliable news, reputable polls, and the market's own implied probability from [[Reading Prices as Probabilities]]. View every market through as neutral a lens as possible.

**Know your edge.** Accurately assess your own expertise in a given domain. You only profit when your estimate of an outcome is better-calibrated than the current market price; without a genuine informational or analytical edge, the price is usually the best available estimate.

**Watch for mispriced tails.** A recurring industry caution is the mispricing of low-probability events, cheap contracts can be over- or under-valued, and that's where both opportunity and traps live.

Strategy is inseparable from [[Risk Management]]: even a good thesis needs position sizing and discipline to survive variance. Match your approach to the structure you're trading from [[Contract Types]].

_This note is educational and is not financial advice._

_Source: https://news.kalshi.com/p/how-prediction-markets-work_

notes.title: Risk Management
notes.body:
# Risk Management

Every trading environment carries risk, financial and informational, and prediction markets are no exception. The educational guidance emphasizes a handful of protective habits.

**Only risk what you can afford to lose.** Treat capital at stake as potentially gone. Contracts can settle worthless, and confident-looking positions still fail.

**Diversify and avoid overexposure.** Committing too much capital to any single market concentrates risk. Spreading across positions cushions the impact of any one being wrong, a direct complement to the position-sizing discipline in [[Strategy & Discipline]].

**Read the rules before you trade.** Each CFTC-regulated market publishes a summary of rules and settlement criteria, including any volume limits. All listed conditions must be met for a contract to pay out, so understanding exact settlement language prevents nasty surprises. Review the platform's terms of service and market rules carefully.

**Control your psychology.** Avoid emotional decisions and cognitive bias. The same neutral, data-first mindset that improves [[Strategy & Discipline]] also limits the impulsive, oversized bets that blow up accounts.

**Know the exits.** On a regulated platform like Kalshi you can cash out accrued funds via bank transfer, debit card, or crypto, but plan your exit as deliberately as your entry.

Risk management ties back to the core insight from [[Reading Prices as Probabilities]]: price reflects probability, and respecting that keeps expectations, and position sizes, realistic.

_This note is educational and is not financial advice._

_Source: https://news.kalshi.com/p/how-prediction-markets-work_

wizards.name: Claude Code Ecosystem
wizards.desc: How Claude Code Skills, Plugins, Marketplaces, and MCP servers differ, install, and fit together.
wizards.author: Lore Library

notes.title: Skills
notes.body:
# Skills

A **skill** is the smallest unit of extension in the Claude Code ecosystem: a single reusable instruction set, packaged as a `SKILL.md` file, that teaches the agent how to do one specific task. Think of it as a focused playbook the model loads on demand, for example a debugging methodology, a code-review checklist, or a communication style.

Key properties:

- **Single-purpose:** each skill targets one task, which keeps it composable and easy to reason about.
- **One-command install:** copy the install command from a directory listing and run it in your terminal; no build step.
- **Model-invoked:** the agent pulls a skill into context when the task matches its description, rather than running constantly.

Skills are the building blocks that larger units assemble. A [[Plugins]] bundle can package several skills together, and a [[Marketplaces]] registry distributes many such bundles. The community directory indexes well over 21,000 skills, sorted by install count, so popularity signals which are battle-tested.

Skills differ from [[MCP Servers]] in an important way: a skill is *instructions* (knowledge and procedure) the model follows, whereas an MCP server adds *tools* (new actions and integrations). Many real workflows combine both, a skill that describes a process plus MCP tools that execute the external calls it needs.

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/llms.txt_

notes.title: Plugins
notes.body:
# Plugins

A **plugin** is a bundle of Claude Code extensions, skills, MCP servers, slash commands, hooks, or agents, packaged together as one installable unit. Where a single [[Skills]] file teaches one task, a plugin groups a coherent set of capabilities so you can install a whole workflow at once.

Why bundles matter:

- **Cohesion:** related pieces ship together, so a methodology and the commands and hooks that support it stay in sync.
- **One install, many capabilities:** instead of wiring up five skills and an MCP server separately, you install the plugin once.
- **Versioning:** the bundle is maintained as a unit, which makes updates and compatibility easier to track.

Plugins sit one level above skills and one level below registries. They are authored and maintained in repositories and then distributed through a [[Marketplaces]] registry, which is how you discover and subscribe to them. A single marketplace can host anywhere from one plugin to over a dozen.

Because a plugin can include [[MCP Servers]] as well as instructions, it is the natural packaging when a capability needs both knowledge and external tools, for example a testing plugin that ships a test-writing skill plus an MCP server that runs the suite. When evaluating a plugin, check how many sub-components it bundles and how it is curated, covered in [[Installing & Curation]].

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/llms.txt_

notes.title: Marketplaces
notes.body:
# Marketplaces

A **marketplace** is a GitHub repository that hosts and distributes multiple plugins under one registry. It is the discovery and distribution layer of the ecosystem: rather than installing one plugin from a random repo, you subscribe to a marketplace and gain access to everything it publishes.

How they work:

- **Subscribe once:** add a marketplace with `/plugin marketplace add <repo>`, then browse and install its plugins.
- **Registry model:** each marketplace acts like a package index, collecting related [[Plugins]] under a single trusted source.
- **Scale varies:** some marketplaces ship a single flagship plugin; others, like the official Anthropic repos, distribute many.

The community directory indexes over 2,500 marketplaces, ranked by GitHub stars and plugin count. Well-known examples include `anthropics/skills`, `anthropics/claude-code`, and community frameworks such as `obra/superpowers`, each curated and actively maintained.

Marketplaces are the top of the packaging hierarchy: a marketplace distributes plugins, a plugin bundles [[Skills]] and [[MCP Servers]], and a skill teaches one task. Choosing a reputable marketplace is itself a quality signal, since maintainers vet what they publish. For how installation and quality ranking work across all of these, see [[Installing & Curation]].

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/llms.txt_

notes.title: MCP Servers
notes.body:
# MCP Servers

An **MCP server** is a Model Context Protocol server that extends Claude Code with external tools, APIs, and integrations. Where a [[Skills]] file adds *knowledge* (how to do something), an MCP server adds *capabilities* (the ability to actually call an external system, query a database, manage a GitHub repo, scrape the web, or read live documentation).

What they provide:

- **Tools, not just instructions:** the server exposes functions the agent can invoke to take real actions.
- **Integrations:** they bridge the agent to services like Supabase, Linear, GitHub, and search/crawling APIs.
- **Standard protocol:** MCP is a common interface, so any compliant server works across MCP-aware agents.

The community directory indexes more than 12,500 MCP servers, ranked by GitHub stars. Popular examples include Context7 (up-to-date code docs), the GitHub server, and Supabase, covered in the companion wizard on standout tools.

MCP servers can be installed on their own or bundled inside a [[Plugins]] package, and distributed through a [[Marketplaces]] registry. The mental model worth keeping: skills are procedures, MCP servers are powers, and the strongest setups combine a skill that knows *what* to do with an MCP server that can *do* it. Quality signals for picking servers are described in [[Installing & Curation]].

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/llms.txt_

notes.title: Installing & Curation
notes.body:
# Installing & Curation

The Claude Code ecosystem is deliberately low-friction to adopt and community-curated for quality.

**Installing:** most items install with a single command. From any detail page you copy the install command and run it in your terminal, no signup, no build step, and it is free to browse and install. A [[Marketplaces]] registry is added once with `/plugin marketplace add <repo>`, after which its [[Plugins]] become available. Individual [[Skills]] and [[MCP Servers]] can also be installed directly.

**How quality is maintained:** the directory ranks and filters extensions by three community signals:

- **Install count**, how many people actually use it.
- **GitHub stars**, durable popularity and trust.
- **Community votes**, direct user endorsement.

Only actively used extensions are listed, and listings are refreshed daily from GitHub via automated discovery, so stale or abandoned tools fall away. This matters because the catalog is huge, over 21,000 skills, 12,500 MCP servers, and 2,500 marketplaces, so ranking by real usage is how you separate signal from noise.

**Practical evaluation tips:** prefer items with high install counts *and* high stars (the two together beat either alone), check the maintainer or marketplace reputation, and remember that a low install count can simply mean a tool is new rather than bad. The directory is an independent project, not affiliated with Anthropic, which is worth noting when weighing endorsements.

_Source: https://crossaitools.com_

wizards.name: Best Claude Code Skills & MCP Servers
wizards.desc: A curated rundown of standout, high-install Claude Code skills, MCP servers, and marketplaces, and when to use each.
wizards.author: Lore Library

notes.title: Skills for Code Quality & Debugging
notes.body:
# Skills for Code Quality & Debugging

The most-installed coding skills focus on disciplined engineering rather than flashy output.

- **systematic-debugging** (obra) — a structured debugging methodology that *mandates root-cause investigation before attempting any fix*. Use it the moment you hit a bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior; it stops the agent from slapping on band-aids. ~124,600+ installs, ~216k stars on its source repo.
- **karpathy-guidelines** (forrestchang) — behavioral guidelines that reduce common LLM coding mistakes by forcing explicit assumptions, simplicity, and verifiable success criteria. Use it during any write/review/refactor to keep changes surgical. ~13,900+ installs.
- **improve** (shadcn) — surveys a codebase as a senior advisor and produces prioritized, self-contained implementation plans for *another* model or agent to execute. Use it to turn a vague "make this better" into an ordered worklist.

These pair naturally: run **improve** to scope the work, lean on **karpathy-guidelines** while editing, and invoke **systematic-debugging** when something breaks. They are *instruction* skills, so combine them with the integrations in [[Dev Platform MCP Servers]] and the broader habits in [[Workflow & Meta Skills]].

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/skills_

notes.title: Workflow & Meta Skills
notes.body:
# Workflow & Meta Skills

Beyond writing code, several top skills shape *how* the agent works and how you discover more capability.

- **grill-me** (mattpocock) — a relentless interviewing skill that stress-tests plans and designs through systematic questioning. Use it before you commit to an approach; it surfaces gaps you would otherwise hit mid-build. ~250,900+ installs, one of the most popular skills in the directory.
- **caveman** (juliusbrussee) — an ultra-compressed communication mode that cuts token usage ~75% while preserving technical accuracy. Use it on long sessions or tight context budgets where verbosity is expensive. ~203,400+ installs.
- **find-skills** (vercel-labs) — discovers and installs specialized skills from the open ecosystem when you need a capability you don't have yet. With ~1,800,000+ installs it is effectively the directory's front door, a meta-skill that bootstraps all the others.

The workflow: use **find-skills** to pull in what you lack, **grill-me** to pressure-test the plan, and **caveman** to stay efficient. These complement the engineering skills in [[Skills for Code Quality & Debugging]] and are distributed through the registries in [[Curated Marketplaces]].

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/skills_

notes.title: Documentation & Context MCP Servers
notes.body:
# Documentation & Context MCP Servers

The single highest-value MCP server for everyday coding is about feeding the agent accurate, current knowledge.

- **Context7** (upstash) — provides up-to-date code documentation for any prompt. Instead of relying on the model's training-cutoff memory of a library, Context7 fetches the current docs for the exact framework, SDK, or API you are using. ~57k stars, among the most-starred MCP servers in the directory.

**When to use it:** any time you ask about a library, framework, SDK, CLI, or cloud service, especially fast-moving ones where APIs change between versions. It directly attacks the most common failure mode of code generation, confidently using an outdated or hallucinated API signature.

**Why it pairs well:** documentation context complements the disciplined-coding habits in [[Skills for Code Quality & Debugging]]; the agent reasons better when it is grounded in real docs rather than guessing. Context7 is an MCP *tool*, so it adds a capability the model can call, distinct from instruction-only skills.

For acting on systems rather than reading about them, see [[Dev Platform MCP Servers]] and [[Web Scraping MCP Servers]]. Context7 itself is also distributed as a plugin through the registries described in [[Curated Marketplaces]].

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/mcp_

notes.title: Dev Platform MCP Servers
notes.body:
# Dev Platform MCP Servers

These MCP servers connect the agent to the platforms where software actually ships, turning natural-language requests into real actions on your tools.

- **GitHub** (github/github-mcp-server) — connects the agent to GitHub to manage repos, issues, pull requests, and workflows through natural language. Use it to triage issues, open PRs, or inspect CI without leaving the conversation. ~30.6k stars.
- **Supabase** (supabase-community) — interacts with the Supabase platform: databases, auth, and project management. Use it when building or operating apps on Supabase so the agent can query and modify backend resources directly. ~2.7k stars.
- **Linear** (app.linear) — project management and issue tracking. Use it to create, update, and link Linear issues so planning and execution stay connected.

**When to reach for these:** any workflow that crosses from "thinking about code" into "operating the systems around it", shipping a PR, checking a table, updating a ticket. They are tool-providing MCP servers, so they complement the instruction skills in [[Skills for Code Quality & Debugging]]. For grounding the agent in accurate docs while it works, combine them with [[Documentation & Context MCP Servers]].

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/mcp_

notes.title: Web Scraping MCP Servers
notes.body:
# Web Scraping MCP Servers

When a task needs data that lives on the open web rather than in your repo or SaaS tools, a scraping MCP server gives the agent eyes on the internet.

- **Scrapling** (d4vinci) — web scraping with stealth HTTP, real browsers, and Cloudflare bypass, with CSS-selector support. Use it to extract structured data from sites that resist naive fetching, pages behind bot protection, JavaScript-rendered content, or anything needing a real browser. ~62.7k stars, one of the most-starred MCP servers in the directory.

**When to use it:** competitive research, pulling pricing or listings, monitoring pages, or any extraction where a plain HTTP GET returns nothing useful because the site renders client-side or blocks scrapers.

**How it fits:** Scrapling is a capability the agent can call, so it complements knowledge-grounding from [[Documentation & Context MCP Servers]] and the platform actions in [[Dev Platform MCP Servers]]. A common pattern is to scrape source data with Scrapling, then have the agent reason over it using the disciplined habits from [[Skills for Code Quality & Debugging]].

Like other servers here, it can be installed directly or via a plugin distributed through [[Curated Marketplaces]].

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/mcp_

notes.title: Curated Marketplaces
notes.body:
# Curated Marketplaces

Marketplaces are GitHub registries that distribute many plugins at once; subscribing to a good one is the fastest way to get a vetted toolkit.

Standout registries by stars and adoption:

- **obra/superpowers** (~137k) — an agentic skills framework and software-development methodology; home of the popular systematic-debugging skill.
- **anthropics/skills** (~111k, 3 plugins) — Anthropic's public repository for Agent Skills, a trustworthy first stop.
- **anthropics/claude-code** (~110k, 13 plugins) — the official Claude Code repo, the largest first-party bundle of plugins.
- **f/prompts.chat** (~158k) — formerly Awesome ChatGPT Prompts; a community library to share, discover, and collect prompts, self-hostable for privacy.
- **affaan-m/everything-claude-code** (~142k) — an agent-harness performance system spanning skills, memory, security, and research-first development across Claude Code, Codex, and more.

**How to choose:** prefer registries with high stars *and* active maintenance, and weight first-party Anthropic repos for baseline trust. A marketplace is a quality filter in itself, since maintainers vet what they publish. From here you install the [[Workflow & Meta Skills]] and [[Documentation & Context MCP Servers]] covered elsewhere in this wizard. Add a marketplace once with `/plugin marketplace add <repo>`, then browse its plugins.

_Source: https://crossaitools.com/marketplaces_
